title: April 2024
excerpt: Learning Java and the beginning of the journey.
content:
My plan at the time was to become very good in my profession: learn an amazing field, get better jobs, and potentially build something of my own.

I started learning Java to follow that path.

title: October 2024
excerpt: Building a chess engine and wanting projects that tell a story.
content:
After I learned the fundamentals in Java, and after exploring a Java framework at work, I decided it's time to make a serious project. Build up a portfolio.

Since I enjoy chess, I started building a chess engine.

In the following months, I spent some time after work on it and I began to find the project more and more interesting. I was increasingly convinced that from now on I want to work on projects like this one. Projects that are interesting but at the same time more personal, and can describe a part of my personality, like enjoying chess.

title: February 2025
excerpt: Turning the chess engine into a full chess web application.
content:
If a project tells a part of my personality, I want people to experience it the way I communicate it.

I decided to turn the chess engine, previously run locally, into a full chess web application, SKGCHESS.

I spend the next months building it with great enthusiasm.

title: April 2025
excerpt: First move over websockets, UI, and learning that appearance and story matter.
content:
For the first time, I had a move sent over WebSockets to the other player and displayed in the frontend. I was very happy to see it working, and next I wanted to shape its appearance to communicate and express myself.

This part took much longer than I expected. I wanted visiting the site to create a specific atmosphere, almost like a small narrative, expressed through the content, the colors, the music, the quotes, and the overall presentation.

I realized that I am more interested in the user experiencing what I create as a narrative than in the technical aspect of the project.

This made me reflect on my work as a software engineer.

Although I am very proud of it, respect it as a field, and enjoy working with people who share that passion — specifically people who are collaborative and committed to producing exceptional work — I realized that I have a need to create things that have meaning for me beyond the programming aspect.

And, as most professional software exists to serve a clear purpose and ultimately create value for a business (even SKGCHESS, if used widely, would be judged as a good online chess app, and fairly so), I came to the conclusion that I cannot satisfy this need by only doing my professional work.

It should be something that the user visits to engage and observe, rather than to use a tool or a functionality.

I kept in mind, though, the possibility of freelancing.

From April until June, I continued refining SKGCHESS to match the way I had imagined it.

If I concluded something from it, it is that I want to work on projects that are more than functionality and can create a narrative you can step into. Not something meant to provide value, but something more abstract that exists for itself.

title: June 2025
excerpt: Two paths (game development or freelancing), first game concept, and the chess club email.
content:
It was time to move on from SKGCHESS. Having understood that it is a personal project that attempted to tell a story to those who visited it, I started thinking in ways that I could continue viewing my work from this perspective, but creating something that people would want to be more invested in and actively engaged, much like we do when commiting to watch a movie or play a game.

I had two ways:

  • Game development
  • Freelancing (I was thinking of trying out indie projects like this where I can express my aesthetics. I was still troubled though, with what type of project could achieve that).

Mid June 2025

The First Game Concept

Having always liked playing games or watching movies, I knew from the moment I started studying Computer Science in university, that game development could be a career I pursue. Specifically solo game development. Before June 2025, I had already tried to play with a game engine, the first time being in winter in 2023, where I just messed up with things, and the second in February of 2025, spending a few days. It's worth noting that the model of the dog appearing in the front page, was made during those days. It is my dog Lilith, as you can see here :)

(image of lilith)

Both times, I thought about game development and tried to examine it but rejected the option. In 2023, I did so because I believed I should first pursue software engineering and try to build a good career in it, and the second time because I was still coming to terms with all the ideas I described earlier.

This third time, I did not open up any engine. Since expression is what I care about, I told myself: if I figure out that I want to tell a story and it has meaning for me, then how to tell the story comes second.

So I wrote down an idea for a scenario. Very roughly: you are inside the mind of a protagonist who is in a state of dormancy, and you are asked to explore its different regions. Each region represents a lost emotion. Afterwards, I started thinking about gameplay mechanics around this idea. For example, the player could have a weapon that fires emotions, and depending on the emotion it shoots, it has different effects on the environment. There are many more ideas, but it's probably better not to expand too much here. I found myself being more attracted, though, to the atmosphere and feeling of the game. I would like something cinematic, thought-provoking, handling existential questions, or philosophizing, while also being fun to play.

Next, I decided that it would be wise to better understand what a game actually is. So I read a book about game design named "A Theory of Fun for Game Design" by Raph Koster. I took away many interesting insights from the book and continued thinking through what I want to create, and whether making a game is the right choice for it.

End June 2025

I received an email about skgchess. I had posted it on LinkedIn some time ago, and a friend from work approached me to help. The whole discussion pushed me to return to the project again and "perfect" it in order to post it when I would be ready, in the website of one of the few local chess clubs in my hometown, Thessaloniki.

title: August 2025
excerpt: Launching IXLSTUDIO as a freelancing page.
content:
SKGCHESS was almost ready for the local chess club. All this effort pushed me to think about starting freelancing, something that is for sure much easier than creating a game.

I launched IXLSTUDIO as my freelancing page. The idea was for people that visit SKGCHESS, to check on IXLSTUDIO and my work too.

I spent the rest of the month and early september, experimenting with the design of the IXLSTUDIO.

title: September 2025
excerpt: Taking a step back, Unreal Engine, painting, Blender, FL Studio, and the big questions.
content:
After returning from vacation, I decided it was time to take a step back.

I had spent more than seven months in what felt like constant exploration, and I needed to slow down a bit.

After resting for some time, I realized I was no longer so sure I wanted to continue with posting SKGCHESS or pursuing freelancing.

I decided to try game development again, this time following a tutorial in Unreal Engine. Since my PC could not run the engine smoothly, I rented the Shadow PC service. Over the next few days, I created some landscapes and experimented with assets. It was genuinely creative and engaging.

This sparked a broader curiosity about art in general — if I were to make a game, I wanted to be able to create my own art in some way. I began painting for the first time, read a book gifted by a good friend, The Story of Art by Gombrich, and started experimenting with Blender and FL Studio for music.

Over the following months, I engaged with these activities from time to time, but I would frequently return to the idea of creating a game, asking myself the same questions:

Do I really want to make a game? Is a game the right medium for what I want to express? If so, what kind of game would I like to make, and what kind of game can I realistically create?

title: January 2026
excerpt: Writing the complete story and the world document.
content:
I came back to what I did in June with the scenario attempt and this time I tried harder. I wrote a complete story, a mixed version of my first scenario, along with new direction. I made a document with the entire history of the world: how it could be delivered narratively, what type of game could help achieve the message, and how the gameplay mechanics could connect with the story of the world.

Knowing that I had a story to tell, the questions that still remained were: What type of game? 3D or 2D? Platformer or shooter? For example, I wanted to make an atmospheric game, perhaps a cinematic one, with clever mechanics. Each desired result is tied to specific genres and perspectives.

The decision between 3D and 2D would also affect the choice of game engine, as Unreal is generally recommended for 3D and Unity for 2D.

title: February 2026
excerpt: Switching to open source and looking into Godot.
content: After moving away from Spotify, I decided more broadly that I want to avoid proprietary software. I switched fully to open source. I reinstalled Linux (which I had used as a student), purged Windows, and with it the Shadow PC service as well. With this shift, I decided that if I were to make a game, it should also be in an open source engine. So I started looking into Godot Game Engine.

title: March 2026 - Date of writing this
excerpt: Starting the game in Godot and Day 1 with the documentation.
content:
The journey of almost a year of constant searching feels like it is finally coming to an end. Along the way, many of these questions were existential, and I changed my perspective on things multiple times. I imagine that every few years in a person's life, something like this happens: a new perspective emerges, and a new chapter begins.

I decided to make a game in Godot, based on the scenario I had written. I don't want to overthink it, so I will start with a 3D first-person game, since I'm mainly interested in 3D, and first-person avoids the animation and camera complexity required for third-person.

A few days ago, I finished ixlstudio in the form you see now. At the same time, I also started teaching some classes related to my profession at a college. My daily job continues to go well. It feels like a good period, where the work of the past year is coming together as this new project begins.

Go to the journal to start following the documentation of making the game.
